title: Spinning Oxford Down wool
subtitle: A soft shortwool breed ideal for beginner-weight yarns
excerpt: Oxford Down is a large British Down breed with a soft, medium-crimped white fleece and a staple of 7-10 cm. Its micron count of approximately 27-32 makes it one of the more forgiving Down fleeces to spin: short enough to manage tension easily, long enough to work from rolags. A reliable breed for those learning to spin from a carded preparation.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from British breed spinning references and Oxford Down Sheep Breeders Association breed notes. Micron and staple figures are representative published ranges. Short-draw from rolags is the standard teaching approach for Down breeds. No single public domain text.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soft roll of carded fibre, ready for spinning. Made by rolling the batt off hand cards; fibres sit at a slight angle rather than fully parallel.
A drafting method where the back hand stays close to the orifice and draws out only a short length of fibre before the wheel takes up the twist. Produces a lofty, woollen-style yarn.
The natural waviness in a wool fibre. More crimps per centimetre generally indicate finer, softer fibre.
The length of a single lock of fleece measured from tip to cut end. It determines which prep method and drafting style will work best.
A length of yarn wound into a loose coil and tied, ready for washing, dyeing, or storing.

content.text: Oxford Down is a large Down breed developed in Oxfordshire from Cotswold and Hampshire Down crosses. Its fleece is white, medium-
, and soft to handle, with a

of 7-10 cm and a micron count in the 27-32 range. This makes it a good beginner breed. The staple is short enough to manage when drafting, but long enough that fibres do not slip out of your grip before the twist arrives.
Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. Tie back hair and roll up sleeves before sitting at the wheel.

, which are the right preparation for the short-draw technique. Start by loading a small pinch of washed Oxford Down onto one card, spreading it evenly across the teeth. Use the second card to brush across the first in short strokes, loading fibres onto the second card gradually. Transfer back and forth three or four times until the fibres sit in a fluffy, even batt. To form the rolag, roll the batt off the teeth of one card from the top down, keeping it loose. Do not compress the roll tightly; the air inside is what makes the short-draw yarn lofty.

Hold the rolag horizontally in your back hand, positioned about 10-12 cm from the orifice of the wheel. Use your front hand to pinch the yarn just ahead of the twist. Draft back a short distance, 5-8 cm, before releasing your pinch and allowing the wheel to draw the twist into the drafting zone. This is the

Keep the treadle rhythm steady and consistent; the amount of twist in the singles is set by the ratio between how fast you treadle and how fast you release fibre to the orifice. Treadle faster for more twist, release fibre faster for less. For a worsted-weight yarn, aim for a twist angle of roughly 30 degrees from the horizontal when you hold a length of singles up to the light.
When your bobbin is full, wind the singles onto the niddy-noddy to form a

. Tie the skein loosely in four places with contrast yarn before sliding it off the niddy-noddy. Soak the skein in warm water for 20 minutes to allow the fibres to relax and the twist to set, then press dry in a towel and hang to dry. A light weight hung from the bottom of the skein while it dries helps the singles lie straight rather than coiling.

Oxford Down spun at this weight will produce a DK to light aran yarn suitable for hats, mittens, and children's garments. The soft crimp structure gives the finished knitting good elasticity and recovery.

items.solution: Either the twist is not reaching the drafting zone before you release your pinch, or the fibre load in the rolag is too thin. Check that you are keeping your pinch firm enough to block the twist, and draft back a shorter distance before releasing. Slightly thicker rolags will also help.

items.problem: The yarn is turning out lumpy with thick and thin sections.
items.solution: The most common cause is inconsistent treadle speed. Keep the wheel turning at a steady rhythm and focus on releasing fibre at the same rate. Practice drafting on a slow ratio before speeding up. Oxford Down is forgiving about lumpiness in the learning stages; the finished skein will even out somewhat during washing.

items.problem: The carded rolags keep falling apart before I can spin them.
items.solution: The rolags need a little more twist in the roll itself. As you roll the batt off the card, twist the roll gently along its length to consolidate it. Do not crush it flat; just enough rotation to hold the fibres together during handling.
